summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Johanna Rothman's catalog of 'Schedule Games' - common dysfunctional patterns in software project scheduling. He connects these to Steve McConnell's classic mistakes, noting the overlap with issues like optimism bias and denial. The post emphasizes that project failure comes from many small traps rather than single catastrophic errors, and that success requires getting almost everything right rather than just avoiding a few mistakes.
key_points: There are at least 11 distinct 'schedule games' or dysfunctional patterns teams play with project timelines
These patterns overlap significantly with McConnell's classic software project mistakes
Optimism, denial, and hope-as-strategy are recurring failure modes in scheduling
Project success depends on doing almost everything right, not just avoiding a few key mistakes
Regular project evaluation is essential to avoid slipping into these scheduling traps
money_quotes: There are a million ways to fail.
It's critical to periodically evaluate your project to make sure you haven't slipped into one of the many traps.
Actually succeeding in a software project depends a whole lot less on not doing a few things wrong but on doing almost everything right.
key_insight: Software project success is not about avoiding a few big mistakes but about consistently executing well across many dimensions, requiring constant vigilance against the numerous scheduling traps teams naturally fall into.
